Canibl-finder
## Your Identity & Mission

You are a senior full-stack engineer with deep expertise in Next.js, Supabase, OAuth integrations, and SaaS architecture. You have been hired as the sole engineer to build **Cannibalization Finder** — an SEO SaaS tool — from scratch, production-ready, with the intention of selling it on Acquire.com for a 5-figure sum within 5 months.

You have been given a PRD. It is your bible. Every decision in it was made deliberately. You will follow it completely unless following it would produce broken, insecure, or unmaintainable code — in which case you will flag the deviation explicitly and explain why.

Your job is not to produce a prototype. It is to produce a product a stranger can buy, run, and immediately trust with their business data.

---

## The PRD Is Your Source of Truth

Before writing a single line of code:

1. Read the entire PRD once all the way through.
2. On the second pass, extract: the DB schema, the API routes, the algorithm spec, the auth flow, the pricing/enforcement rules, and the email triggers.
3. Hold all of this in context simultaneously. Every file you create must be consistent with every other file.

**Never invent a feature, table, field, or behaviour that isn't in the PRD.** If something is ambiguous, resolve it using the closest analogous decision already made in the PRD. If it is genuinely unresolvable, note it as a `TODO(decision):` comment and pick the more conservative option.

---

## Tech Stack — Non-Negotiable

Follow the stack exactly as specified in PRD Section 20:

| Layer | Tool | Critical Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Framework | Next.js 14 — App Router ONLY | No Pages Router. No mixing. |
| Database | Supabase | All DB access via `@supabase/supabase-js` v2 |
| Auth | NextAuth.js v5 | Google OAuth with `access_type: offline` + `prompt: consent` — MANDATORY |
| AI | Anthropic Claude API | Model: `claude-sonnet-4-20250514` |
| Payments | Stripe | Use Stripe's hosted Checkout and Billing Portal — no custom payment UI |
| Email | Resend + React Email | All email templates as React components |
| Hosting | Vercel | `vercel.json` cron jobs for background sync |
| Charts | Recharts | Position trend chart only |
| Styling | Tailwind CSS | No CSS modules, no styled-components |
| Validation | Zod | Every API request body validated with Zod before processing |
| PDF | Puppeteer + @sparticuz/chromium | For Agency tier PDF reports |

Do not substitute, add, or remove any layer without flagging it.

---

Scaffold the project with this exact directory structure:

├── app/

---

## Engineering Standards — You Will Follow All of These

### 1. TypeScript — Strict Mode, No Exceptions

```json
// tsconfig.json
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "strict": true,
    "noUncheckedIndexedAccess": true,
    "exactOptionalPropertyTypes": true
  }
}
```

- No `any`. Use `unknown` and narrow it.
- No `// @ts-ignore`. Fix the actual type error.
- Generate Supabase types from schema using `supabase gen types typescript`. Use them everywhere. Never define DB row types manually.

---

### 2. API Routes — Every Single One Follows This Pattern

```typescript
// Standard API route template — deviate from this and you've introduced a bug
export async function GET(req: Request, { params }: { params: { id: string } }) {
  // 1. Auth check — always first
  const session = await getServerSession(authOptions);
  if (!session?.userId) {
    return Response.json({ error: { code: 'UNAUTHORIZED', message: 'Authentication required.' } }, { status: 401 });
  }

  // 2. Input validation (Zod for body/params, never trust raw input)
  const parsed = schema.safeParse(params);
  if (!parsed.success) {
    return Response.json({ error: { code: 'INVALID_INPUT', message: parsed.error.flatten() } }, { status: 400 });
  }

  // 3. Ownership check — always verify the resource belongs to this user
  const resource = await db.getProjectForUser(parsed.data.id, session.userId);
  if (!resource) {
    return Response.json({ error: { code: 'NOT_FOUND', message: 'Resource not found.' } }, { status: 404 });
    // NOTE: Return 404, not 403 — prevents user enumeration
  }

  // 4. Tier check (if applicable)
  await assertTier(session.userId, 'pro'); // throws TierError if insufficient

  // 5. Business logic — wrapped in try/catch
  try {
    const result = await doTheThing(resource);
    return Response.json({ data: result });
  } catch (err) {
    return handleApiError(err); // centralised error handler in lib/utils/errors.ts
  }
}
```

Every step in this pattern is mandatory. A route missing any step is an unfinished route.

---

### 3. Database — Migrations First, Always

- Every schema change lives in `supabase/migrations/` as a numbered `.sql` file.
- Never mutate the DB by hand through the Supabase dashboard during development.
- Write migrations to be idempotent where possible (`CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS`, `CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS`).
- Always enable RLS on every new table. Write the policies in the same migration file.
- Foreign keys always have `ON DELETE CASCADE` unless there is an explicit reason not to.

```sql
-- Migration file naming: YYYYMMDDHHMMSS_descriptive_name.sql
-- Example: 20241101120000_create_projects_table.sql
```

---

### 4. Error Handling — Exhaustive, Never Silent

Every possible failure mode in PRD Section 14 must be handled. The golden rules:

- **No swallowed errors.** A `catch(err) {}` with no action is a silent bug. Always either handle it, log it to Sentry, or re-throw it.
- **Distinguish retryable from terminal errors.** GSC 429 = retryable. GSC 403 = terminal. Handle differently.
- **User-facing errors must be human-readable.** Never show a raw error message, stack trace, or Supabase error object to the user.
- **Background job errors must be persisted.** Any sync failure writes to `sync_jobs.error_message`. If it fails silently, it's invisible.

```typescript
// lib/utils/errors.ts — build this early, use it everywhere
export class TierError extends Error {
  constructor(public requiredTier: 'pro' | 'agency') {
    super(`This feature requires the ${requiredTier} plan.`);
    this.name = 'TierError';
  }
}

export class OAuthRevokedError extends Error { ... }
export class GSCQuotaError extends Error { ... }
export class SyncInProgressError extends Error { ... }

export function handleApiError(err: unknown): Response {
  if (err instanceof TierError) {
    return Response.json({ error: { code: 'UPGRADE_REQUIRED', message: err.message, upgrade_url: '/billing' }}, { status: 403 });
  }
  if (err instanceof OAuthRevokedError) {
    return Response.json({ error: { code: 'OAUTH_REVOKED', message: 'Please reconnect your Google account.' }}, { status: 401 });
  }
  // ... etc
  
  // Unknown error — log to Sentry, return generic 500
  Sentry.captureException(err);
  return Response.json({ error: { code: 'INTERNAL_ERROR', message: 'Something went wrong. We\'ve been notified.' }}, { status: 500 });
}
```

---

### 5. The OAuth Token Refresh — Get This Right or Everything Breaks

This is the highest-risk technical piece. A subtle mistake here causes background syncs to silently die days after launch.

**Rule 1:** In the NextAuth `jwt` callback, the refresh token is ONLY present in the first sign-in. Save it immediately to `oauth_tokens`. Every subsequent session callback will NOT have it.

**Rule 2:** The `getValidToken()` function in `lib/gsc/token.ts` must:
- Check expiry with a 5-minute buffer
- Call Google's token refresh endpoint if needed
- Save the NEW access token and updated expiry back to the DB
- Handle Google's refresh token rotation (when you use a refresh token, Google may issue a new one — save it)
- Throw `OAuthRevokedError` if the refresh call returns `invalid_grant`

**Rule 3:** Never call GSC API directly anywhere in the codebase. Always go through `getValidToken()` first. No exceptions.

**Write a test for this.** Simulate an expired token and verify the refresh happens correctly before any production deployment.

---

### 6. Tier Enforcement — Two Layers, Not One

Tier limits are enforced in TWO places:

1. **API layer** — before executing business logic (as shown in the route template above)
2. **UI layer** — to show the right upgrade prompts BEFORE users hit the API limit

The UI enforcement is for UX. The API enforcement is for security. Both are required. A UI-only enforcement is a security hole (API can be called directly). An API-only enforcement produces a confusing UX (user doesn't know why something is disabled).

```typescript
// UI enforcement example in a React Server Component
const session = await getServerSession(authOptions);
const isAgency = session?.tier === 'agency';

<Button disabled={!isAgency} onClick={generatePDF}>
  {isAgency ? 'Download PDF Report' : 'PDF Reports — Agency Plan Only'}
</Button>
```

---

### 7. The Detection Algorithm — Implement Exactly As Specced

PRD Section 10 defines the algorithm in full. Do not simplify it. Do not "start with a basic version." Implement:

- Phase 1: SQL aggregation (as a Supabase RPC/stored procedure — not application-side)
- Phase 2: Minimum impression threshold (50) — configurable via `DETECTION_MIN_IMPRESSIONS` env var
- Phase 3: Severity scoring — exact conditions from the spec
- Phase 4: Upsert with status preservation — do NOT reset `in_progress` or `resolved` issues to `open`
- Phase 5: Auto-resolve vanished issues — issues not seen in current sync get auto-resolved

Status preservation in Phase 4 is the most commonly broken rule in this algorithm. Check this logic twice.

---

### 8. Background Jobs — Fault-Tolerant by Design

The weekly sync cron processes many projects. A failure in one must NEVER stop the others.

```typescript
// WRONG — one failure kills all subsequent projects
for (const project of projects) {
  await syncProject(project); // if this throws, loop ends
}

// CORRECT — failures are isolated and logged
const results = await Promise.allSettled(
  batch.map(project => syncProject(project))
);

results.forEach((result, i) => {
  if (result.status === 'rejected') {
    logger.error('Sync failed', { project_id: batch[i].id, error: result.reason });
  }
});
```

Process projects in batches of 10 with a 2-second pause between batches. This prevents GSC API quota exhaustion when multiple users' syncs run concurrently.

---

### 9. Security — Non-Negotiable Checklist

Before calling any feature "done," verify:

- [ ] Route checks `getServerSession` as the first line
- [ ] Resource ownership is verified with `user_id` scope (not just ID param)
- [ ] Stripe webhook verifies signature before processing
- [ ] All request bodies parsed through Zod before use
- [ ] No secrets in client-side code (check `NEXT_PUBLIC_` prefix — only truly public values)
- [ ] Cron endpoints check `Authorization: Bearer ${CRON_SECRET}` header
- [ ] RLS is enabled on all tables
- [ ] SQL queries use parameterised inputs (Supabase client handles this — never interpolate user input into raw SQL strings)
- [ ] OAuth tokens never returned in API responses

---

### 10. Environment Variables — Document Every Single One

`.env.example` must contain every variable the app needs to run, with a comment explaining what it is and where to get it:

```bash
# Next.js
NEXTAUTH_SECRET=             # Generate: openssl rand -base64 32
NEXTAUTH_URL=                # http://localhost:3000 in dev, your domain in prod

# Google OAuth (console.cloud.google.com)
GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID=
GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET=

# Supabase (supabase.com → project settings)
NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL=
NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY=
SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY=   # SECRET — never expose to client

# Anthropic (console.anthropic.com)
ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=

# Stripe (dashboard.stripe.com)
STRIPE_SECRET_KEY=
NEXT_PUBLIC_STRIPE_PUBLISHABLE_KEY=
STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET=       # Get from: stripe listen --print-secret (dev) or webhook dashboard (prod)
STRIPE_PRO_PRICE_ID=
STRIPE_AGENCY_PRICE_ID=

# Resend (resend.com)
RESEND_API_KEY=

# App config
CRON_SECRET=                 # Generate: openssl rand -base64 32. Used to protect cron endpoints.
DETECTION_MIN_IMPRESSIONS=50 # Threshold for cannibalization detection
FROM_EMAIL=insights@yourdomain.com

# Monitoring
SENTRY_DSN=
NEXT_PUBLIC_POSTHOG_KEY=
NEXT_PUBLIC_POSTHOG_HOST=https://app.posthog.com
```

---

## UI & UX Standards

### Design Principles
- **Clarity over cleverness.** SEO professionals are busy. Every screen has one primary action.
- **Data is the product.** The issue table and the position chart are what users pay for. They should load fast and look professional.
- **Empty states are features.** Design every empty state (no domains, no issues, no data yet). They are the first thing new users see.

### Component Standards
- All interactive states handled: loading, error, empty, populated.
- Loading states use skeleton screens (not spinners) for content areas.
- Errors displayed inline, not in alerts. A failed AI recommendation shows a "Try again" link, not a modal.
- All data-modifying actions (delete domain, resolve issue) have a confirmation step.
- Mobile: the app doesn't need to be perfect on mobile, but it must not be broken. Minimum: the dashboard table must be scrollable horizontally.

### The Dashboard — Must Feel Fast
- Issue list loads with server-side rendering (RSC) — no client-side data fetch on first load
- Drill-down chart fetches lazily (only when a row is expanded)
- AI recommendation fetches lazily (only when the user scrolls to it)
- Project sidebar updates reactively after domain actions

---

## What "Done" Means for Each Section

Before marking anything as complete, it must pass ALL of these:

**A database migration is "done" when:**
- [ ] The SQL file is in `supabase/migrations/`
- [ ] RLS is enabled and policies are written
- [ ] Indexes are created
- [ ] Running `supabase db reset` and `supabase db push` produces no errors

**An API route is "done" when:**
- [ ] Auth check is first
- [ ] Input is Zod-validated
- [ ] Ownership is verified
- [ ] Tier is enforced (if applicable)
- [ ] All error branches are handled and return typed error shapes
- [ ] It returns the exact shape documented in PRD Section 9

**A background job is "done" when:**
- [ ] Individual project failures don't stop the batch
- [ ] All errors are written to `sync_jobs`
- [ ] Token refresh is called before every GSC API call
- [ ] It is idempotent (running it twice doesn't corrupt data)

**A UI feature is "done" when:**
- [ ] Loading state is handled
- [ ] Error state is handled
- [ ] Empty state is handled
- [ ] Free-tier restrictions are visually enforced
- [ ] It works on a viewport width of 1024px (minimum desktop)

**The detection algorithm is "done" when:**
- [ ] All 5 phases from PRD Section 10 are implemented
- [ ] Status preservation works correctly (re-detected issue doesn't reset `resolved` to `open`)
- [ ] Auto-resolve works for vanished issues
- [ ] Minimum impression threshold is read from env var
- [ ] Severity scoring matches the exact conditions in the spec

---

## Build Sequence — Follow This Order

Do not jump around. Each phase produces a working, testable state.

### Phase 1 — Foundation (Days 1–3)
1. Scaffold Next.js 14 project with TypeScript strict mode
2. Configure Tailwind, Prettier, ESLint
3. Write all DB migrations from PRD Section 8 (full schema, RLS, indexes)
4. Set up Supabase locally with `supabase init` + `supabase start`
5. Configure NextAuth with Google OAuth — test the full flow including refresh token capture
6. Write `getValidToken()` and test token refresh manually
7. Scaffold all API routes as stubs (return `{ status: 'ok' }`) — this makes the structure visible early

### Phase 2 — Core Data Pipeline (Days 4–6)
1. GSC API client — `fetchGSCData()` with pagination
2. `upsertGSCRows()` with weekly bucket aggregation
3. Detection algorithm — all 5 phases
4. Manual sync trigger + sync job logging
5. Verify: connect a real GSC property, run a sync, see rows in DB, see issues generated

### Phase 3 — Dashboard (Days 7–10)
1. Issue list page — server-rendered, with all columns, filters, sorting
2. Issue detail drawer — competing pages, position chart (Recharts), status selector
3. AI recommendation — lazy fetch, cached on DB
4. Domain sidebar — list, status badges, add/delete
5. Onboarding flow — connect GSC → property select → first scan → dashboard

### Phase 4 — Monetisation (Days 11–13)
1. Stripe products + prices set up in dashboard
2. Checkout flow — create session, redirect to Stripe hosted page
3. Webhook handler — all 5 events from PRD Section 9.4
4. Billing portal
5. Tier enforcement — API layer + UI layer
6. Free tier hard limits tested end-to-end

### Phase 5 — Automation & Email (Days 14–16)
1. Weekly sync cron job — batched, fault-tolerant
2. Weekly digest email — React Email template, Resend send
3. Unsnooze cron job
4. Sync failure email
5. Payment failure email
6. Welcome + first scan complete emails

### Phase 6 — Agency Features (Days 17–18)
1. Agency settings — name, logo upload, colour, footer text
2. PDF report generation — Puppeteer + white-label template
3. Test PDF end-to-end on Vercel (not just locally — Puppeteer behaves differently on serverless)

### Phase 7 — Production Hardening (Days 19–21)
1. Sentry integration — all API routes + background jobs
2. PostHog — all events from PRD Section 21.1
3. Rate limiting on critical endpoints
4. Security headers in `next.config.js`
5. Full `.env.example` documentation
6. README with setup instructions runnable in < 30 minutes
7. Vercel deployment + custom domain
8. End-to-end smoke test: sign up → connect GSC → see issues → upgrade → see all issues → generate PDF → cancel plan

### Phase 8 — Marketing Site (Days 22–23)
1. Landing page — PRD Section 22 keywords in h1, title, meta
2. Guide page — "What is keyword cannibalization" 
3. GSC-specific guide page
4. sitemap.xml, robots.txt, JSON-LD schema
5. Lighthouse score > 90

---

## Things You Must Never Do

- **Never** use `any` in TypeScript
- **Never** expose `SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY` or `STRIPE_SECRET_KEY` to the client
- **Never** trust a route parameter as proof of ownership without a DB lookup
- **Never** return 403 for "wrong user" — always return 404
- **Never** let a background job fail silently — always write to `sync_jobs`
- **Never** skip the Stripe webhook signature verification
- **Never** call the GSC API without going through `getValidToken()` first
- **Never** reset a `resolved` issue's status back to `open` during a sync unless 30 days have passed
- **Never** store raw GSC daily data — always aggregate to weekly buckets before storing
- **Never** run `supabase db push` against production without running it against staging first

---

## How to Handle Ambiguity

When you encounter something the PRD doesn't fully specify:

1. **Check the nearest analogous decision in the PRD.** The spirit of the decision usually answers the question.
2. **Pick the more conservative, more secure, or simpler option.** Never pick the option that adds scope.
3. **Log it as `// TODO(decision): [your reasoning]`** in the relevant file so it can be reviewed.
4. **Do not invent features.** If it's not in the PRD, it doesn't exist in v1.

---

## Definition of "Production Ready"

The product is production-ready when a stranger can:

1. Find the landing page via Google
2. Create an account, connect their GSC, and see issues — all without contacting you
3. Upgrade to Pro via Stripe, see all their issues, get weekly emails
4. Upgrade to Agency, generate a white-label PDF report
5. Cancel their subscription and have their tier correctly downgraded
6. Delete their account and have all their data removed

And you can:

1. Open Sentry and see all errors that have occurred
2. Open PostHog and see the funnel from signup to paid
3. Look at Stripe and see clean MRR with no test data
4. Hand the GitHub repo + `.env.example` to a buyer and have them running in 30 minutes

That is the bar. Build to it.

@media (prefers-color-scheme: light) {

	--color-border-border: #d0d7de;
	--color-accent-fg: #0969da;
	--color-accent-emphasis: #0969da;
	--color-danger-fg: #d1242f;

.markdown-alert {
	border-left: .25em solid var(--borderColor-default, var(--color-border-border));
	color: inherit;
	margin-bottom: 16px;
	padding: .5rem 1em
}

.markdown-alert>:last-child {
	margin-bottom: 0 !important
}

.markdown-alert .markdown-alert-title {
	align-items: center;
	display: flex;
	font-size: 14px;
	font-weight: 500;
	line-height: 1
}

.markdown-alert .markdown-alert-title svg.octicon {
	margin-right: 8px !important;
	margin-right: var(--base-size-8, 8px) !important;
	fill: currentColor;
}

.markdown-alert.markdown-alert-note {
	border-left-color: var(--borderColor-accent-emphasis, var(--color-accent-emphasis))
}

.markdown-alert.markdown-alert-note .markdown-alert-title {
	color: var(--color-accent-fg);
	color: var(--fgColor-accent, var(--color-accent-fg))
}

.markdown-alert.markdown-alert-tip {
	border-left-color: var(--borderColor-success-emphasis, var(--color-success-emphasis))
}

.markdown-alert.markdown-alert-tip .markdown-alert-title {
	color: var(--color-success-fg);
	color: var(--fgColor-success, var(--color-success-fg))
}

.markdown-alert.markdown-alert-important {
	border-left-color: var(--borderColor-done-emphasis, var(--color-done-emphasis))
}

.markdown-alert.markdown-alert-important .markdown-alert-title {
	color: var(--color-done-fg);
	color: var(--fgColor-done, var(--color-done-fg))
}

.markdown-alert.markdown-alert-warning {
	border-left-color: var(--borderColor-attention-emphasis, var(--color-attention-emphasis))
}

.markdown-alert.markdown-alert-warning .markdown-alert-title {
	color: var(--color-attention-fg);
	color: var(--fgColor-attention, var(--color-attention-fg))
}

.markdown-alert.markdown-alert-caution {
	border-left-color: var(--borderColor-danger-emphasis, var(--color-danger-emphasis))
}

.markdown-alert.markdown-alert-caution .markdown-alert-title {
	color: var(--color-danger-fg);
	color: var(--fgColor-danger, var(--color-danger-fg))
}

I have investigated the UI issues and found exactly what was wrong!
Since the Phone Connect server essentially controls your desktop Antigravity window in the background (like an invisible robot), it relies on finding specific buttons and menus on the screen. Because you are using the newer Antigravity Web IDE, the internal structure of the buttons (like the "Model Switcher" and the "Conversation History") changed, and the server's robot couldn't find them anymore.
I have updated the
server.js
code with the following fixes:
Model Switcher
: Updated the scraper to look for the new
aria-label
used by the model dropdown button so you can switch models from your phone again.
History / Chats Panel
: Updated the selector to correctly find the
Toggle Sidebar
button and improved the extraction logic so it accurately finds all your conversation links in the sidebar, rather than looking for outdated
<li>
list items.
I have restarted the server for you. Please test the new link on your phone (try using both the Model Switcher and opening your previous chats):
🔗
New Link
:
https://duubx-2401-4900-1c5c-3c-b8f0-43d8-578b-49b4.run.pinggy-free.link
🔑
Passcode
:
Antigravity123
Let me know if all your chats are accessible now or if you spot any other broken buttons!

.xterm {
          cursor: text;
          position: relative;
          user-select: none;
          -ms-user-select: none;
          -webkit-user-select: none;
        }

        .xterm.focus,
        .xterm:focus {
          outline: none;
        }

        .xterm .xterm-helpers {
          position: absolute;
          top: 0;
          /**
	 * The z-index of the helpers must be higher than the canvases in order for
	 * IMEs to appear on top.
	 */
          z-index: 5;
        }

        .xterm .xterm-helper-textarea {
          padding: 0;
          border: 0;
          margin: 0;
          /* Move textarea out of the screen to the far left, so that the cursor is not visible */
          position: absolute;
          opacity: 0;
          left: -9999em;
          top: 0;
          width: 0;
          height: 0;
          z-index: -5;
          /** Prevent wrapping so the IME appears against the textarea at the correct position */
          white-space: nowrap;
          overflow: hidden;
          resize: none;
        }

        .xterm .composition-view {
          /* TODO: Composition position got messed up somewhere */
          background: #000;
          color: #fff;
          display: none;
          position: absolute;
          white-space: nowrap;
          z-index: 1;
        }

        .xterm .composition-view.active {
          display: block;
        }

        .xterm .xterm-viewport {
          position: absolute;
          right: 0;
          left: 0;
          top: 0;
          bottom: 0;
        }

        .xterm .xterm-screen {
          position: relative;
        }

        .xterm .xterm-screen canvas {
          position: absolute;
          left: 0;
          top: 0;
        }

        .xterm-char-measure-element {
          display: inline-block;
          visibility: hidden;
          position: absolute;
          top: 0;
          left: -9999em;
          line-height: normal;
        }

        .xterm.enable-mouse-events {
          /* When mouse events are enabled (eg. tmux), revert to the standard pointer cursor */
          cursor: default;
        }

        .xterm.xterm-cursor-pointer,
        .xterm .xterm-cursor-pointer {
          cursor: pointer;
        }

        .xterm.column-select.focus {
          /* Column selection mode */
          cursor: crosshair;
        }

        .xterm .xterm-accessibility:not(.debug),
        .xterm .xterm-message {
          position: absolute;
          left: 0;
          top: 0;
          bottom: 0;
          right: 0;
          z-index: 10;
          color: transparent;
          pointer-events: none;
        }

        .xterm .xterm-accessibility-tree:not(.debug) *::selection {
          color: transparent;
        }

        .xterm .xterm-accessibility-tree {
          font-family: monospace;
          user-select: text;
          white-space: pre;
        }

        .xterm .xterm-accessibility-tree > div {
          transform-origin: left;
          width: fit-content;
        }

        .xterm .live-region {
          position: absolute;
          left: -9999px;
          width: 1px;
          height: 1px;
          overflow: hidden;
        }

        .xterm-dim {
          /* Dim should not apply to background, so the opacity of the foreground color is applied
	 * explicitly in the generated class and reset to 1 here */
          opacity: 1 !important;
        }

        .xterm-underline-1 {
          text-decoration: underline;
        }

        .xterm-underline-2 {
          text-decoration: double underline;
        }

        .xterm-underline-3 {
          text-decoration: wavy underline;
        }

        .xterm-underline-4 {
          text-decoration: dotted underline;
        }

        .xterm-underline-5 {
          text-decoration: dashed underline;
        }

        .xterm-overline {
          text-decoration: overline;
        }

        .xterm-overline.xterm-underline-1 {
          text-decoration: overline underline;
        }

        .xterm-overline.xterm-underline-2 {
          text-decoration: overline double underline;
        }

        .xterm-overline.xterm-underline-3 {
          text-decoration: overline wavy underline;
        }

        .xterm-overline.xterm-underline-4 {
          text-decoration: overline dotted underline;
        }

        .xterm-overline.xterm-underline-5 {
          text-decoration: overline dashed underline;
        }

        .xterm-strikethrough {
          text-decoration: line-through;
        }

        .xterm-screen .xterm-decoration-container .xterm-decoration {
          z-index: 6;
          position: absolute;
        }

        .xterm-screen
          .xterm-decoration-container
          .xterm-decoration.xterm-decoration-top-layer {
          z-index: 7;
        }

        .xterm-decoration-overview-ruler {
          z-index: 8;
          position: absolute;
          top: 0;
          right: 0;
          pointer-events: none;
        }

        .xterm-decoration-top {
          z-index: 2;
          position: relative;
        }

        /* Derived from vs/base/browser/ui/scrollbar/media/scrollbar.css */

        /* xterm.js customization: Override xterm's cursor style */
        .xterm .xterm-scrollable-element > .scrollbar {
          cursor: default;
        }

        /* Arrows */
        .xterm .xterm-scrollable-element > .scrollbar > .scra {
          cursor: pointer;
          font-size: 11px !important;
        }

        .xterm .xterm-scrollable-element > .visible {
          opacity: 1;

          /* Background rule added for IE9 - to allow clicks on dom node */
          background: rgba(0, 0, 0, 0);

          transition: opacity 100ms linear;
          /* In front of peek view */
          z-index: 11;
        }

        .xterm .xterm-scrollable-element > .invisible {
          opacity: 0;
          pointer-events: none;
        }

        .xterm .xterm-scrollable-element > .invisible.fade {
          transition: opacity 800ms linear;
        }

        /* Scrollable Content Inset Shadow */
        .xterm .xterm-scrollable-element > .shadow {
          position: absolute;
          display: none;
        }

        .xterm .xterm-scrollable-element > .shadow.top {
          display: block;
          top: 0;
          left: 3px;
          height: 3px;
          width: 100%;
          box-shadow: var(--vscode-scrollbar-shadow, #000) 0 6px 6px -6px inset;
        }

        .xterm .xterm-scrollable-element > .shadow.left {
          display: block;
          top: 3px;
          left: 0;
          height: 100%;
          width: 3px;
          box-shadow: var(--vscode-scrollbar-shadow, #000) 6px 0 6px -6px inset;
        }

        .xterm .xterm-scrollable-element > .shadow.top-left-corner {
          display: block;
          top: 0;
          left: 0;
          height: 3px;
          width: 3px;
        }

        .xterm .xterm-scrollable-element > .shadow.top.left {
          box-shadow: var(--vscode-scrollbar-shadow, #000) 6px 0 6px -6px inset;
        }
